{"step_by_step_thinking": "Myasthenia gravis is characterized by fluctuating muscle weakness that worsens with activity and improves with rest. Common presenting features include ptosis (drooping of the eyelids), diplopia (double vision), and muscle fatigability. These symptoms are due to impaired neuromuscular transmission caused by autoantibodies against acetylcholine receptors.

Ptosis (Option A) is a classic and common initial symptom of myasthenia gravis, as noted in multiple documents (e.g., Document [0], [9], [14]).

Muscle fatigability (Option B) is a hallmark feature, as muscles weaken with repeated use and improve with rest (Document [9], [25], [31]).

Pupillary reflex (Option D) is described as normal in myasthenia gravis. Document [17] explicitly states: 'The pupils, deep tendon reflexes, and sensory examinations are normal.' This indicates that pupillary reflexes are preserved.

Deep tendon reflexes (DTRs) are examined in the physical exam. However, the documents do not report absent DTRs as a feature of myasthenia gravis. In fact, the physical examination may reveal normal muscle power due to the fluctuating nature of the disease, and DTRs are specifically mentioned as being normal (Document [17]). Absent DTRs are not described or implied in any of the documents as a feature of myasthenia gravis. This makes Option C (Absent DTRs) the exception.

Therefore, absent DTRs are not seen in myasthenia gravis, while ptosis, muscle fatigability, and normal pupillary reflex are all characteristic.", "answer_choice": "C"}
